top_findings.finding: Participant identified that the website lacks text-to-speech functionality, a feature they know others need for accessibility.
top_findings.observed_signal: Participant stated the absence of a read-aloud option and noted this is important for people who need it, though not a disability they personally have.
top_findings.stated_signal: I don't know, it doesn't really have an option anywhere for things to be read out loud. Um, that's not a particular disability I have, but, um, I do know people who like to be able to hear things out loud. Um, so if there was something I could click that could read it out for me, that would be great.
top_findings.rationale: The participant explicitly identified a missing accessibility feature (text-to-speech) that would benefit users with specific access needs. This is a significant accessibility barrier even though the participant themselves did not require it for task completion. The stated concern reflects genuine friction in the product's accessibility offerings, meeting S3 (significant component failure in accessibility design).
top_findings.structural_amplification_note: This friction is structurally amplified for users who rely on text-to-speech, screen readers, or audio-based content consumption. Blind/low-vision and neurodivergent users may be particularly affected by the absence of this feature.

top_findings.finding: Participant expressed concern that the current placement of accessibility content would be difficult for users with visibility or accessibility issues.
top_findings.observed_signal: Participant recommended relocating content to the top in bold, explicitly stating it would help people with accessibility issues find and access it more easily.
top_findings.stated_signal: As someone who has can sometimes have visibility issues and I have trouble finding things. This is not as clear... I would definitely recommend it's right at the top in bold somewhere, either on the left or the right corner, because the people who have accessibility issues would definitely have trouble finding it.
top_findings.rationale: The participant identified that accessibility content is not discoverable in its current location and would pose a barrier to users with visibility or accessibility issues. This is a structural usability problem affecting a key user cohort; the participant's frustration and specific recommendations indicate significant friction in locating critical accessibility resources.

top_findings.finding: Participant recalled difficulty locating the accessibility information on Bupa, which was placed low on the page and required significant search effort.
top_findings.observed_signal: Participant noted that accessibility content on Bupa was located at the bottom and was not easy to find, contrasting it with Aviva where accessibility is positioned higher up.
top_findings.stated_signal: When I did my original accessibility search on Bupa, it took me a while to find it, whereas [on Aviva it is higher up and easier to find].
top_findings.rationale: The participant explicitly stated that finding accessibility information on Bupa required significant time and effort ('took me a while'), indicating a past but meaningful wayfinding friction event. The comparison to the current site suggests the issue was resolved through better information architecture, but the stated difficulty on the prior product demonstrates high friction prior to recovery.
top_findings.structural_amplification_note: This friction is particularly acute for accessibility-conscious users or those relying on assistive technology who need to verify accessible features early in the evaluation process.

top_findings.finding: Participant encountered unresponsiveness or multiple-click requirement when attempting to select an option with foot mouse.
top_findings.observed_signal: Participant clicked the dropdown option multiple times before it registered; expressed uncertainty whether the issue was device or website related.
top_findings.stated_signal: That hasn't seemed to have worked for me. I've had to click it multiple times. I'm not sure whether that was, um, an issue with my foot mouse or with the website.
top_findings.rationale: The participant's stated and observed signals align: multiple clicks were required for a single interaction to register, creating high friction. Although the task ultimately succeeded, the repeated-attempt pattern and expressed uncertainty match the S4 gate (multiple failed attempts before recovery) and L3 (significant friction with eventual completion).
top_findings.structural_amplification_note: Foot mouse users experience amplified friction from unresponsive interfaces, as each repeated click requires additional deliberate motor effort and introduces latency uncertainty.

top_findings.finding: Clicking on the 'Prevent constipation' link resulted in a 'requested document was not found' error page.
top_findings.observed_signal: Participant clicked on the 'Prevent constipation' link and encountered a broken or missing page.
top_findings.stated_signal: When I did click on Prevent constipation. It took me to the requested document was not found.
top_findings.rationale: The participant attempted to navigate to content via a link but encountered a 404 or 'not found' error, which is a content pathway failure. The participant recovered by trying an alternative link ('fiber rich foods'), so the overall task was not blocked. This represents a single, recoverable friction event with mild negative sentiment.

coaching_recommendations.title: Review high-friction moments before lower-level issues
coaching_recommendations.summary: The session contains 27 finding-level issue(s), with the highest severity at S3.
advice: Identify the common pattern behind the higher-severity findings.
Focus coaching on the most disruptive parts of the session first.
Avoid treating all findings as equal when severity levels differ.
coaching_recommendations.evidence_note: 5.1-A severity distribution: S3=1, S4=3, S5=23. Representative findings: Participant identified that the website lacks text-to-speech functionality, a feature they know others need for accessibility. | Participant expressed concern that the current placement of accessibility content would be difficult for users with visibility or accessibility issues. | Participant recalled difficulty locating the accessibility information on Bupa, which was placed low on the page and required significant search effort.

coaching_recommendations.title: Multi-pattern friction across 7 categories — anchor coaching on the dominant pattern
coaching_recommendations.summary: This session shows 27 findings spanning 7 distinct friction types (F6,F5,F4,F3,F1,F2,F7). Coaching should follow the dominant pattern first rather than issuing one item per type.
advice: After addressing any severity-priority recommendation first, use this aggregation to group the remaining coaching themes — do not treat the two as parallel.
Treat F6 (Not Found) as the primary coaching anchor; secondary types (F5 Unexpected, F4 Unresponsive) provide supporting context.
Avoid issuing parallel recommendations for each friction type when patterns interact within the same session.
coaching_recommendations.evidence_note: 5.1-A: total=27 findings across 7 distinct friction types (F6=8, F5=6, F4=4, F3=3, F1=2, F2=2, F7=2).
